@graph.name: BMW X5 xDrive40d Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 xDrive40d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X5 xDrive40d engine typically costs £4,500–£9,500 supply only depending on generation, with the current B57D30B sitting at £5,500–£9,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. BMW engine replacement costs typically range from £2,500 to over £10,000 depending on the engine model. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 xDrive40d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation you're asking about. The N57 (F15, 2013–2018) is generally reliable but turbocharger failures, timing chain stretch, and oil leaks have been reported. The B57 (G05, 2019+) is widely regarded as a reliable unit, but turbocharger failures, oil leaks, and EGR system issues have been reported. Across both, oil-change discipline is everything.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 xDrive40d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Turbocharger failures (particularly between 100,000–150,000 km), oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing, DPF clogging (especially on urban-driven cars), EGR system failures (B57), and fuel injector issues. See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 xDrive40d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept F15 X5 xDrive40d (values £8,000–£18,000+), a £5,000–£6,000 reconditioned engine is 25–60% of the vehicle's value — often worth it for a solid example. Against a £20,000–£50,000 G05, a £5,500–£6,500 recon is 10–30% — worth it. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 xDrive40d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: F15 (2013–2018) = N57D30; G05 (2019–present) = B57D30B. The B57 engine features BMW's TwinPower Turbo technology with a two-stage turbocharging system. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the turbocharger, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Turbocharger failure is repairable without replacing the engine — if caught early. A turbocharger replacement costs £1,500–£3,500. However, if the turbo fails catastrophically and sends debris into the engine, a full engine replacement (£4,500–£9,500) may be required. At the first sign of whistling or power loss, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW X5 xDrive40d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current X5 xDrive40d range. Older generations: see the Era Map above for F15/N57 and G05/B57-specific pricing. BMW engine replacement costs typically range from £2,500 to over £10,000 depending on the engine model.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X5 xDrive40d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The X5's engine bay is reasonably accessible, but the diesel straight-six adds some complexity. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

cta.label: → Compare X5 xDrive40d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Turbocharger Failures (N57 & B57 — F15, G05)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, whistling or whining noise from the engine, excessive exhaust smoke, reduced fuel economy, engine warning light, "Drivetrain Malfunction" warning.
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£7,500 (if turbo failure sends debris into the engine)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear whistling or experience power loss, book an inspection immediately

problems.recommendation: The powerful diesel engines in the X5 40d typically show turbocharger problems between 100,000 and 150,000 km. The B57 engine features a two-stage turbocharging system with variable turbo geometry, which adds complexity. Turbocharger failures can lead to a loss of power and increased exhaust smoke. If you notice a whistling sound or loss of power, have the boost system inspected immediately. Regular oil changes with the correct BMW-approved oil and allowing the engine to cool after hard driving are the best prevention.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Valve Cover Gasket & Oil Filter Housing — N57 & B57)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage around the valve cover or oil filter housing, low oil level warning.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,000 (valve cover gasket or oil filter housing gasket replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are among the most frequently reported issues on both N57 and B57 engines. They commonly occur from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing. Oil leaks can lead to low oil levels and potential engine damage if not addressed promptly. The B57 engine is also known for oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing. If you notice oil spots under the car or a burning smell, have it inspected promptly.

problems.title: Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) Clogging (N57 & B57)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced power, increased fuel consumption, frequent regeneration cycles, smoke during acceleration.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles (urban-driven cars are at higher risk)
problems.repairCost: £200–£800 (DPF clean, forced regeneration, or sensor replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can lead to costly emissions system damage

problems.recommendation: Diesel X5 models, particularly the 30d and 40d, can develop soot build-up in the DPF or EGR system if driven mostly on short trips. Failed DPF regeneration won't be covered by warranty if caused by driving habits. Some owners have reported DPF blockage causing high exhaust pressure and failed regenerations. If you see a DPF warning light, take the car for a sustained motorway run at 2,000–2,500 rpm. If the light persists, have the DPF inspected. Regular motorway driving and using premium diesel can help keep the DPF healthy.

problems.title: EGR System Failures (B57 — G05)
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, loss of power, rough idle, increased fuel consumption, increased emissions.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless coolant loss leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if warning light appears

problems.recommendation: The EGR system can become clogged or fail, leading to increased emissions and potential engine performance issues. On short-distance driven cars, the EGR system is particularly prone to clogging. BMW has issued recalls for EGR cooler failures on some diesel applications. If you notice a loss of power or a warning light, have the EGR system inspected promptly.

problems.title: Fuel System Issues (Fuel Injectors & Fuel Pumps)
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, decreased fuel efficiency, loss of power, engine misfires, check engine light.
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£7,500 (if fuel system failure leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough idling or loss of power occurs

problems.recommendation: Problems with the fuel injectors and fuel pumps can lead to rough idling and decreased fuel efficiency. The B57 engine uses common rail piezo direct fuel injection capable of up to 10 injections per cycle, with fuel pressure of up to 2,700 bar — this high-pressure system can be expensive to repair. Some drivers have experienced issues with fuel injectors, leading to rough idling and decreased fuel efficiency. Regular fuel filter changes and using high-quality diesel are the best prevention.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW X5 xDrive40d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Viable if caught before debris enters engine
Cleaning or forced regeneration is cost-effective
Recall work may be available; out-of-warranty repair is still viable
Replacement is straightforward; catch it before fuel system damage occurs
Both N57 and B57 are robust; catastrophic failures are rare but expensive

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X5 xDrive40d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–10,000 mile intervals (not BMW's extended CBS schedule)
* F15 (2013–2018): no turbo noise and oil leaks addressed
* G05 (2019+): no EGR or DPF warning lights
* Regular motorway use (DPF will be healthier than an urban-only car)
* No cold-start rattles (timing chain should be healthy)
avoidIf: * No proof of regular oil changes — these engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Turbo whistling or power loss present — expensive turbo work is imminent
* Any DPF or EGR warning lights — emissions system repairs can be costly
* Oil leaks present — valve cover or oil filter housing may be failing
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — these engines can hide expensive faults

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW X5 xDrive40d is the performance diesel flagship of the X5 range, offering effortless straight-six power and refinement across two generations. The F15's N57 is a proven powerhouse but demands turbocharger and timing chain vigilance. The G05's B57 is a significant step forward in performance and efficiency, with the added benefit of mild-hybrid technology, but EGR, DPF and turbocharger issues need attention. Across both generations, one rule applies: oil-change discipline is everything. BMW's extended service intervals are widely considered inadequate — treat your X5 xDrive40d to 8,000–10,000 mile oil changes and it will reward you with hundreds of thousands of miles of effortless motoring.

cta.label: → Compare X5 xDrive40d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 xDrive40d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattles (N57 timing chain warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (N57/B57) and confirmed oil-change discipline (8k–10k miles max)
* Turbo Health — Any whistling, power loss, or smoke? (turbo wear warning)
* DPF Health — Any warning lights? Ask about regeneration frequency; urban cars will have more issues
* EGR System — Any warning lights or loss of power? Check recall status
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the valve cover and oil filter housing
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Turbo, EGR, DPF, or fuel system codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (DPF, EGR, NOx sensor issues)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows exactly match Section 1B's Era Map — the same engine codes, years, and generation coverage.

items.label: * Fuel System
items.value: Common rail piezo direct injection (up to 2,700 bar)

items.label: * Turbocharger
items.value: Two-stage turbocharging with variable turbo geometry for high- and low-pressure stages

items.label: * Mild Hybrid
items.value: 48V system with 11 PS (11 hp) starter generator

items.label: * Transmission
items.value: 8-speed Steptronic automatic with xDrive all-wheel drive
